body_txt: Like so many tropes, this one exists for one reason: it's cool . You don't have to be an arrow scientist to figure out that arrows go at crazy speeds. Very few people in the world can catch arrows, and the archer has to help them in order to make it practical, but when they do do it, it's pretty darn cool. James Randi (a.k.a. The Amazing Randi) discussed this, since, among the many, many things he did in his life, he also studied archery. Most arrows used in this trick are called a flu-flu; an arrow with much larger fletching designed for hunting small birds. The arrow will leave the bow at high speed, but at a certain distance will slow down dramatically so that you can retrieve your arrow if you miss. Those who catch arrows do so after they have slowed, but before people can see that they have done so. In other words, don't try this at home unless you've got the right equipment. Even then, if you're not careful, you're still trying to catch an arrow and could still be injured. It can be justified in some cases by giving a character an advantage such as Super-Reflexes thanks to a innate ability or Charles Atlas Superpower . It's a good way for the Badass Normal to show off in comparison to the truly superpowered or just the general badass in works where everybody's going medieval on each other. This trope is also a very common trick for Bare-Fisted Monks and other martial artsy types, to show off their dexterity; as a consequence, these kinds of characters may have special defenses or counters against ranged weapons in Role-Playing Games and related media. Beware the Trick Arrow , however! More than one would-be badass has been undone when a seemingly-normal arrow has exploded in their hands or suddenly turned into a snake. See Bullet Catch for this trope with bullets instead of arrows. See Catch and Return for this trope plus a follow-up move.
